explicit_text: I was living in the Village of Westmont, Illinois when I decided to join the Army in 1965. At the time, I was working construction and a friend and I were thinking about military service as the Vietnam War was heating up. Westmont was home for me as I prepared to take on what I thought would be a great adventure.

explicit_text: I enlisted in the United States Army at age 20 in 1965, motivated by a friend who was a paratrooper and the emerging reputation of the Special Forces. I was inducted in Chicago and went through basic training at Fort Knox, Kentucky. The Army was a place where all kinds of men came together, and it was about becoming a soldier regardless of background.

explicit_text: My uncle served in World War II, and my father was a railroad engineer during that time, so he was exempt from military service. While I didn't have direct family in the military except my uncle, their experiences influenced my respect for service. World War II was a backdrop to my family's history as I prepared to join the Army during Vietnam.

explicit_text: I was inducted into the Army in Chicago in 1965, at an induction center downtown possibly near Dearborn Street. My friends and I left Chicago by train from Union Station heading to Fort Campbell, Kentucky. Chicago was the place where my military journey officially began.

explicit_text: After induction, I went to Fort Knox, Kentucky for basic training in August 1965. The barracks were old World War II style with coal-fed furnaces, and the training was tough with strict discipline and daily inspections. Fort Knox was where I transformed from a civilian into a soldier, learning to live and work under Army standards.
